1: Kata epangelian zōēs tēs en Christō Iēsou (according to the promise of life that is in Christ Jesus): the apostolate is grounded in and oriented toward a divine promise. Epangelia (promise) — the covenantal promise of life; the qualifier "in Christ Jesus" locates the promise not in general religious hope but in the specific person and history of the Messiah. Unlike Romans 1:1 (set apart for the gospel) or Galatians 1:1 (not from humans), the qualification here is eschatological: Paul's apostleship is defined by its goal — the life promised and now manifest in Christ.
2: Gnēsiō teknō (genuine/true child): gnēsios = legitimate, not spurious — the same adjective used in Philippians 4:3 for the "loyal yokefellow." In the ancient world a gnēsios child was one of recognized legitimacy within the family, as opposed to a spurious or adopted child. Timothy's apostolic legitimacy as Paul's heir is signaled by this single adjective. The triad charis, eleos, eirēnē (grace, mercy, peace) expands the standard Pauline greeting (grace + peace) with eleos (mercy) — the specifically OT covenantal-loyalty term (hesed) that appears in the Pastoral Epistles' greetings but not Paul's other letters.
3: Hō latreuō apo progonōn en katharā syneidēsei (whom I serve from forebears with clean conscience): latreuō is specifically priestly-cultic worship language (used for service at the temple and to God), applied here to Paul's apostolic ministry — his entire life of ministry is priestly service. Apo progonōn (from forebears/ancestors) — Paul's service to God is continuous with his Jewish heritage, not a break from it; he worships the God of his ancestors in Christ. Katharā syneidēsei (clean/clear conscience) — syneidēsis (conscience) is prominent in the Pastoral Epistles (1 Timothy 1:5, 19; 2 Timothy 3:9; 2 Timothy 4:2; Titus 1:15); a clear conscience is not innocence but the result of living consistently with what one knows to be true.
4: Epipothō se idein (I long to see you): epipotheō is a strong longing-verb (the prefix epi- intensifies the desire); it appears in Philippians 1:8 for Paul's intense longing for the Philippians. The personal warmth of the closing section of 2 Timothy is striking — this is the most intimate of the Pastoral Epistles. Tōn dakryōn sou (your tears) — probably the tears at their last parting; the memory of Timothy's emotional farewell is the spur to Paul's longing. Hina charas plērōthō (so that I may be filled with joy) — the joy of reunion that would complete what the tears began.
5: Hypomnēsin labōn tēs en soi anypokritou pisteōs (having received/taken a reminder of the sincere faith in you): hypomnēsis = the act of calling to mind, a reminder that reactivates memory. Anypokritus (sincere, unhypocritical) — a- + hypokritēs (actor, one who plays a part); sincere faith is contrasted with performed faith. The same adjective appears in 1 Timothy 1:5 for love that is unhypocritical. Prōton en tē mammē sou Lōidi kai tē mētri sou Eunikē (first in your grandmother Lois and your mother Eunice) — the feminine names are Jewish diaspora names; the multigenerational faith chain is the personal embodiment of the Deuteronomic transmission mandate (Deuteronomy 6:7).
6: Anazōpyrein to charisma tou theou (to fan into flame / rekindle the gift of God): anazōpyreō — ana (again/up) + zōpyron (live coal, ember); the compound suggests rekindling a dying or banked fire. The image is not of a fire that has gone out but of one whose coals need fanning to full flame. Charisma (gift of grace) — the specific gift given at ordination; what is "in" Timothy through the laying on of Paul's hands (dia tēs epitheseōs tōn cheirōn mou). The gift can apparently diminish in vigor without being lost — hence the call to fan it actively rather than assume it burns automatically.
7: Pneuma deilian (spirit of cowardice/timidity): deilia — the only NT occurrence; deilos (coward) appears in Matthew 8:26 and Revelation 21:8. The spirit of cowardice is contrasted with the spirit of dynamis (power), agapē (love), and sōphronismos (self-discipline/sound-mindedness). Sōphronismos — related to sōphrōn (sound-minded, self-controlled), a major virtue in the Pastoral Epistles; here in its abstract noun form (only here in NT). The Pastoral Epistles' characteristic triad of virtues — power, love, and disciplined soundness of mind — maps closely onto the Stoic virtues but is grounded in the divine gift rather than human cultivation.
8: Mē oun epaischynthēs to martyrion tou Kyriou hēmōn (do not therefore be ashamed of the testimony of our Lord): epaischynomai (to be ashamed) — the shame-avoidance verb connects to the honor-shame dynamics of the Greco-Roman world; Paul has just declared he is not ashamed (2 Timothy 1:12). The call not to be ashamed presupposes that social pressure toward shame is real — imprisonment and public humiliation attach to Paul as stigmata. Synkakopathēson (suffer with me / share in suffering) — the compound verb (syn + kakopatheo) makes suffering a partnership; Timothy is invited into co-participation in Paul's imprisonment-sufferings. Kata dynamin theou (according to the power of God) — suffering is done in reliance on divine power, not human endurance.
9: Sōsantos hēmas kai kalesantos klēsei hagia (who saved us and called us with a holy calling): two aorist participles summarize the entire divine initiative — saving and calling are prior acts that precede Timothy's and Paul's response. Ou kata ta erga hēmōn alla kata idian prothesis kai charin (not according to our works but according to his own purpose and grace) — prothesis (purpose/intention; literally the setting-forth of something); the divine purpose is the ground of salvation. Pro chronōn aiōniōn (before eternal times) — the pre-temporal dimension of grace: given before time began, now revealed in Christ's appearing. The structure anticipates the Ephesians 1 framework of pre-temporal divine election.
10: Katargēsantos men ton thanaton (having abolished/nullified death): katargeō = to render inoperative, to nullify, to make of no effect; Paul uses the same verb in 1 Corinthians 15:26 (the last enemy to be abolished is death) and 1 Corinthians 2:6 (the rulers of this age being abolished). Death is not destroyed in the sense of ceasing to exist but is rendered powerless — stripped of its former dominion. Phōtisantos de zōēn kai aphtharsian (but illuminating life and immortality) — the gospel as a light that illuminates (makes visible) what was previously hidden in darkness: life (zōē) and incorruptibility (aphtharsia). Epiphaneia (appearing) — the Pastoral Epistles' signature Christological term for both the incarnation (here) and the Parousia (2 Timothy 4:1, 8; 1 Timothy 6:14; Titus 2:13).
11: Kēryx kai apostolos kai didaskalos (herald and apostle and teacher): the three-fold ministerial designation. Kēryx (herald) — specifically the public crier who makes official proclamations; the most basic description of Paul's proclamatory function. Apostolos (apostle) — the commissioned envoy sent with the authority of the sender. Didaskalos (teacher) — the one who provides systematic instruction. The three titles cover the full range: proclamation (kēryx), authority and sending (apostolos), and sustained instruction (didaskalos). Elsewhere Paul uses herald (kēryx) language only in 1 Timothy 2:7 and here — it is a more public-rhetorical term than "apostle."
12: Oida gar hō pepisteukai (for I know whom I have believed/trusted): the perfect tense pepisteukai (I have believed and continue believing) describes the settled, completed act of trust that Paul stands in. Hō (whom) — the object of trust is a person, not a proposition. Kai pepeismai hoti dynatos estin tēn parathēkēn mou phylaxai (and I am convinced that he is able to guard my deposit): here parathēkē mou = what Paul has deposited with God — his life, ministry, and ultimate welfare. This is the reverse of 1:14 where parathēkē is what God has deposited with Timothy. Paul's confidence in suffering is grounded in the reliability of the one who holds his deposit in trust.
13: Hypotyōsin echōn hygiainontōn logōn hōn par' emou ēkousas (hold the pattern/sketch of sound words which you heard from me): hypotypōsis (pattern, outline, sketch) — the architectural or artistic term for a preliminary sketch or model from which the finished work is constructed; the sound words Paul taught are the hypotypōsis Timothy holds as his working model. Hygiainontōn logōn (of sound/healthy words) — the medical metaphor (hygiainō = to be healthy) characteristic of the Pastoral Epistles. En pistei kai agapē tē en Christō Iēsou (in faith and love that are in Christ Jesus) — the sphere in which the pattern is held and transmitted.
14: Tēn kalēn parathēkēn phylaxon dia pneumatos hagiou (guard the good deposit through the Holy Spirit): the imperative phylaxon (guard) contrasts with the indicative phylaxai in 2 Timothy 1:12 — God guards Paul's deposit; Timothy is commanded to guard the deposit entrusted to him. The agent of guarding is the Holy Spirit who dwells in us — the imperative is not a solo effort but a Spirit-enabled fidelity. The double use of parathēkē in 2 Timothy 1:12 and 14 creates a theological chiasm: what Paul entrusts to God (2 Timothy 1:12) is the model for what God entrusts to Timothy (2 Timothy 1:14).
15: Apestraphēsan me pantes hoi en tē Asia (all those in Asia turned away from me): apostrephō = to turn the face away, to avert; the gesture of social disavowal. "All in Asia" is a broad rhetorical statement for the widespread desertion of Paul at his arrest; the named examples (Phygelus and Hermogenes) are not otherwise known. Asia (the Roman province) was Paul's primary ministry area — Ephesus its capital. The desertion is not necessarily apostasy but the social abandonment of a shamefully imprisoned man; association with a prisoner endangered the reputation and safety of one's household.
16: Anepsyxen me (he refreshed me): anapsychō = literally to cool, give air to breathe; the compound of ana (again) + psychō (to breathe, cool); a hapax in the NT. The physical imagery of giving air to breathe in the stifling context of Roman imprisonment is acute — Onesiphorus's visits provided literal relief in a hot, airless cell. Ouk epaischynthē tēn halysis mou (not ashamed of my chain) — the single chain (halysis, singular) attached a prisoner to a guard; Onesiphorus's willingness to visit publicly acknowledged association with a shamed prisoner.
17: Alla genomenon en Romē spoudaiōs ezētēsen me kai heuren (but when in Rome he searched diligently for me and found me): the aorist narrative of deliberate effort — spoudaiōs (diligently, eagerly) combined with ezētēsen (he searched) and heuren (he found). Roman imprisonment in the first century did not have a fixed location; prisoners awaiting trial might be kept in various places depending on their legal status and social connections. Onesiphorus's search through the city for Paul suggests some difficulty in locating the specific prison or custody situation.
18: Dōē autō ho Kyrios heurein eleos para Kyriou en ekeinē tē hēmera (may the Lord grant that he find mercy from the Lord on that day): the optative dōē (may he grant) expresses a prayer-wish; the repetition "Lord... from the Lord" is notable — some see a reference to God the Father and the Lord Jesus as the eschatological judge (cf. 2 Timothy 4:8: "the Lord, the righteous Judge"), making this a Trinitarian-inflected prayer. En ekeinē tē hēmera (on that day) — the Day of Judgment, the eschatological day of reckoning that the Pastoral Epistles consistently look toward (2 Timothy 1:12; 2 Timothy 4:8). The personal concern for Onesiphorus's eschatological welfare matches the depth of personal gratitude expressed in 2 Timothy 1:16-17.
